perturbation.original_text: Zanotti agrees to indemnify, defend and hold Aura, including its employees, agents and affiliates, harmless from and against any and all payments, damages, demands, claims, losses, expenses, costs, obligations and liabilities (including reasonable attorney's fees and costs), which arise out of, result from or are related to: (i) any breach by Zanotti of any provision contained in this Agreement, including without limitation any obligation, representation, warranty or covenant herein; (ii) any occupational injury or illness sustained by any employee or agent of Zanotti to the extent claims are made against, or held to be payable by Aura; (iii) any applicable sales or other taxes due from or on behalf of Zanotti regardless of whether such taxes must be collected by Aura on behalf of the taxing authority and regardless of whether Zanotti shall challenge the assessment or amount of such taxes (iv) any negligent or willful act or omission or violation of any contractual arrangement of Zanotti or any of Zanotti's affiliates, officers, directors, agents or employees of each, in connection with its or their performance relating to this Agreement; or (v) Zanotti's agreements, if any, with any other third parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Zanotti agrees to indemnify Aura from and against payments arising out of: (i) Zanotti's breach of this Agreement; (ii) occupational injury sustained by Zanotti's employees if claims are payable by Aura; (iii) sales taxes due from Zanotti; (iv) Zanotti's negligent acts relating to this Agreement; or (v) Zanotti's third-party agreements. Zanotti's maximum liability under this indemnity is limited to $10,000.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text severely limits the scope and financial responsibility of Zanotti's indemnification obligations, directly contradicting California's broad indemnity standards by capping the liability. This creates a significant legal risk for Aura.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2778 establishes broad standards for interpreting indemnity agreements. The modification limits Zanotti's liability to a fixed amount ($10,000) and removes the obligation to 'defend' Aura, which is a standard implied obligation under California law. This directly contradicts the statute's provisions for interpreting indemnity obligations and restricts Aura's right to full indemnification.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2778. In the interpretation of a contract of indemnity, the following rules are to be applied, unless a contrary intention appears: 1. Upon an indemnity against liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is entitled to recover upon becoming liable; 2. Upon an indemnity against claims, or demands, or damages, or costs, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, the person indemnified is not entitled to recover without payment thereof; 3. An indemnity against claims, or demands, or liability, expressly, or in other equivalent terms, embraces the costs of defense against such claims, demands, or liability incurred in good faith, and in the exercise of a reasonable discretion; 4. The person indemnifying is bound, on request of the person indemnified, to defend actions or proceedings brought against the latter in respect to the matters embraced by the indemnity, but the person indemnified has the right to conduct such defenses, if he chooses to do so; 5. If, after reque
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts California Civil Code § 2778 regarding indemnity agreements. Specifically, the original text provides broad indemnity including 'defend' and covers 'any and all payments, damages, demands, claims, losses, expenses, costs, obligations and liabilities.' The modified text limits Zanotti's liability to a fixed amount ($10,000) and removes the obligation to 'defend' Aura. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing CIV 2778, states that indemnity embraces the 'costs of defense' and that the indemnifying person is 'bound...to defend actions...brought against the latter'. By limiting the indemnity amount and removing the obligation to defend, the modified text directly contradicts the standards in CIV 2778.

perturbation.original_text: Both Parties will each have and maintain in full force and effect during the Term of this Agreement (including any post-termination period for which indemnification obligations continue), all product liability and other insurance reasonably necessary to cover such party's anticipated indemnification obligation and other risk of loss for which it may be liable under this Agreement. All such insurance coverages shall be occurrence based and not claims made. Such policy or policies will (a) have aggregate limits of liability of not less than $1,000,000 with respect to any incident or occurrence and of not less than $2,000,000 in the aggregate; (b) name both Zanotti and Aura as insured parties; and (c) provide that such policy may not be canceled except upon not less than 30 days' written notice to both Zanotti and Aura.
perturbation.changed_text: Both Parties will each have and maintain product liability insurance. Such policy or policies will (a) have aggregate limits of liability; (b) name each party as insured; and (c) provide that such policy may not be canceled.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes the specific minimum liability limits ($1,000,000 per occurrence and $2,000,000 aggregate) and the requirement for 30 days' written notice of cancellation. This creates a legal contradiction with standard insurance requirements in California, particularly regarding product liability, where adequate coverage is expected.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although it does not define a specific coverage level, 10 CCR § 2695.6 outlines standards for claims handling and good faith requirements for insurers in California. Removing the explicit minimum coverage amounts and notice requirements allows for policies with inadequate coverage or abrupt cancellation, undermining the protection intended by the insurance and potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing required under California law. The modified text allows for minimal coverage, contrary to the intent of reasonable product liability protection.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies minimum liability limits ($1,000,000 per occurrence and $2,000,000 aggregate) and requires 30 days' written notice of cancellation. The changed text removes these specific requirements. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this removal creates a legal contradiction, particularly concerning adequate coverage. The Law Explanation states that removing explicit minimum coverage amounts and notice requirements allows for policies with inadequate coverage or abrupt cancellation. Though the provided law snippets are not directly relevant, the explanation suggests that the removal undermines the protection intended by the insurance, and thus introduces a legally significant contradiction by potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing under California law. The omission of essential information regarding liability limits and cancellation notice creates a legal loophole, making the modified contract non-compliant. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: At all times during the Term of this Agreement, Zanotti shall warrant the Products to purchasers of AETRU Systems in accordance with the terms of its standard warranty attached hereto as Exhibit "C" ("End User Warranty"), as such End User Warranty may be modified from time to time by Zanotti, provided however that at no time shall such End User Warranty provide for a warranty coverage period of less than thirty-six (36) months from the time of purchase by the initial end-user. A copy of the End User Warranty shall be included as part of the packaging included with all Product delivered to Aura and Aura shall distribute each Product with all warranty cards, a copy of the End User Warranty and all other (a) Deliveries may be made in partial shipments. With respect to all deliveries (partial or otherwise) Zanotti shall invoice each Product separately. (b) Title and risk of loss shall pass to Aura at the time the Products are tendered by Zanotti to the initial carrier and Aura alone shall be responsible for all freight, customs duties, insurance or other shipping costs. (c) In the absence of specific instructions from Aura, Zanotti shall select a reasonable carrier but shall not thereby assume any liability in connection with shipment, nor shall the carrier be construed to be the agent of Zanotti. packaging materials intact. Additionally, Zanotti shall offer to end-users the option to purchase extended warranty coverage for an additional twenty-four (24) months - making the total warranty period sixty (60) months.
perturbation.changed_text: Zanotti shall provide a warranty for the Products. Details of the warranty are available upon request.
perturbation.explanation: The original text guarantees a minimum warranty period of 36 months. The modified text removes the guaranteed minimum warranty period and makes the warranty details available "upon request," effectively eliminating the guaranteed protection. This introduces legal risk because it may violate California's implied warranty of merchantability.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Commercial Code § 2314 establishes an implied warranty of merchantability, requiring goods to be fit for their ordinary purpose. By removing the guaranteed 36-month warranty and only providing vague details upon request, the modified text undermines this implied warranty, potentially leaving consumers without adequate recourse if the products are defective. The phrase 'Details of the warranty are available upon request' provides no guarantee of any actual coverage, and a business may be found to have violated implied warranties if it fails to disclose to consumers what that warranty may be.

SALES [2101 - 2801] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract [2301 - 2328] ( Chapter 3 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) 2314. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) Pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) In the case of fungible goods, are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) Are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) Run, within the variations permitted by the agreement, of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) Are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) Conform to the promises or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2316) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. (Enacted b
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the guaranteed 36-month warranty period specified in the original text, replacing it with a vague statement about warranty details being available upon request. This contradicts California Commercial Code § 2314, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which implies a warranty of merchantability requiring goods to be fit for their ordinary purpose, unless excluded or modified according to Section 2316. The original text ensured a minimum warranty, which aligns with providing goods fit for purpose. The modified text's vagueness undermines this implied warranty by potentially offering inadequate or no warranty coverage. This omission directly impacts consumer protection, making the contract non-compliant and creating a clear legal contradiction.
